No Poverty.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course syllabus directly relates to poverty eradication, economic resources for the poor, or social protection systems.

Zero Hunger.reason: The reading 'Cosmopolitan Locavorism: Global Local-food Movements' directly relates to sustainable food systems and consumption, aligning with Target 2.4.
evidence: Cosmopolitan Locavorism: Global Local-food Movements in Postcolonial Hong Kong

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The course extensively covers 'psychological therapies,' 'well-being,' 'Medical Anthropology,' 'Treatment & Healing,' and 'Care,' directly addressing mental health and well-being (Target 3.4).
evidence: a growing demand for psychological therapies
subjective and objective conditions of well-being

Quality Education.reason: The course emphasizes 'cross-cultural comparison,' 'critical thinking,' and cultivating 'care for society,' aligning with Target 4.7's focus on knowledge for sustainable development and cultural diversity.
evidence: cultivate students’ ability to care for society and themselves

Gender Equality.reason: The inclusion of 'Feminist Anthropology' and discussions on 'Kinship, Relationships and Relatedness' provide an explicit, albeit minor, link to understanding gender dynamics and equality (Target 5.c).

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course syllabus relates to water quality, sanitation, or hygiene.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course syllabus relates to energy access, renewable energy, or energy efficiency.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The 'Cosmopolitan Locavorism' reading offers a very tenuous, inferred link to promoting local culture and products (Target 8.9), which can indirectly support economic growth.

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course syllabus relates to industrialization, infrastructure development, or technological innovation.

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course's focus on 'dignity,' 'values,' and 'cross-cultural comparison' directly contributes to understanding and promoting social inclusion (Target 10.2) across diverse groups.

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The 'Local-food Movements in Postcolonial Hong Kong' case study provides an inferred, minor link to safeguarding cultural heritage within communities (Target 11.4).
evidence: Cosmopolitan Locavorism: Global Local-food Movements in Postcolonial Hong Kong

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The 'Cosmopolitan Locavorism: Global Local-food Movements' reading directly addresses consumption patterns and sustainable lifestyles, aligning with Target 12.8.

Climate Action.reason: The inclusion of 'Environmental Anthropology' as a sub-field provides an inferred link to understanding human-environment interactions and climate adaptation (Target 13.1).

Life Below Water.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course syllabus relates to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or sustainable fisheries.

Life on Land.reason: The 'Environmental Anthropology' sub-field and 'healing practices of the Peruvian Amazon' reading infer a link to the sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems and biodiversity (Target 15.1).
evidence: healing practices of the Peruvian Amazon

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course's explicit focus on 'ethics,' 'dignity,' 'empathy,' and 'care for society' provides a strong foundation for understanding and promoting just societies (Target 16.3, 16.b).

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: As an 'International Doctoral Program' utilizing 'cross-cultural comparison,' the course inherently promotes global knowledge sharing and capacity-building (Targets 17.6, 17.9).
evidence: International Doctoral Program in Asia-Pacific Studies
